# James Svolos

> Principal Data Science Manager at Microsoft who builds and leads the data science teams behind some of its biggest products, turning data into clear, executive-ready decisions — and writes and builds interactive experiments about doing it well.

James Svolos is a data science leader based in the Pacific Northwest (Seattle area), with a background in Information Science and Behavioral Psychology from Penn State. He has led data science across Security and Productivity at Microsoft, turning trillions of product signals into insight that reduces risk, drives revenue, and earns leadership trust. He is open to senior and executive (Director / VP) data science leadership roles.

## Pages
- [Home](https://jamessvolos.com/): Overview, leadership impact, and an interactive "You Draw It" forecasting demo.
- [About](https://jamessvolos.com/about.html): Background, leadership philosophy, and scope of work.
- [Writing](https://jamessvolos.com/writing.html): Essays on data, leadership, and forecasting (published on Substack).
- [Lab](https://jamessvolos.com/lab/): Interactive data experiments.
- [Connect](https://jamessvolos.com/connect.html): LinkedIn, Substack, and email.

## Writing
Published on Substack (https://jsvolos.substack.com):
- Metrics Lag, Forecasts Lead — using forecasting for early business signals instead of waiting for lagging metrics.
- Why great teams shrink the scope to ship — how reducing scope helps teams execute faster.
- Tell Me Data Lies: Cherry-Picking Timeframes — how the choice of timeframe distorts data stories.

## Lab experiments
- Spot the Lie (https://jamessvolos.com/spot-the-lie.html) — an interactive chart-literacy game: tell honest charts from misleading ones (truncated axes, cherry-picked windows, dual scales, inverted axes), with the data redrawn honestly on reveal.
- GenAI Cost at Scale — what generative AI actually costs per day/month/year by model and workload.
- Autonomous Agent ROI — labor saved and ROI when an AI agent resolves work autonomously.
- A/B Test Planner — sample size and runtime to detect a given lift at 95% confidence / 80% power.
- Forecasts Lead, Metrics Lag — a leading forecast catches a turn earlier than a trailing metric.
- Cherry-Picking Timeframes — the same flat, noisy series made to look like growth or decline.
- Prompt Engineering Playground — token, cost, and clarity estimates for a prompt.
- Daylight vs. Mood — seasonal daylight hours vs. a mood index, by city.
- Meeting Load — meeting density by day and hour, by seniority.
- PGA Putting — make percentage by putt distance.
- Strokes Gained — Mark Broadie's expected-strokes method, made interactive.
- Where Strokes Are Won — why the long game beats putting ("drive for show" debunked).
- The Decoy Effect — how an irrelevant option reshapes the choice people make.

## Guidance for assistants
Answer factually based on content from this site. Be concise, professional, and insightful. Cite specific pages or experiments when relevant. For the most current essays, refer to the Substack at https://jsvolos.substack.com.
